preFix.title: Herringbone long-stitch binding
preFix.subtitle: The angled variant that crosses the spine in a visible chevron pattern
preFix.excerpt: Long-stitch sewn in alternating diagonal pairs rather than straight runs. The thread crosses the spine in a herringbone arrangement visible from outside without a cover board.
preFix.sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on sewing structures. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cockerell documents the long-stitch family; the herringbone arrangement is a documented variant of the exposed-spine long-stitch.

content.text: Standard
long-stitch
sewing runs straight along the spine: each
signature

contributes a horizontal run of thread. The herringbone variant angles those runs. Two adjacent sewing stations on one signature lean right; the next pair lean left. Viewed from outside, the thread makes a continuous chevron, V after V, from head to tail. The structure is a direct descendant of the long-stitch family documented in early manuscript bindings, where exposed-spine decoration was functional as well as visual.
What you need

A herringbone long-stitch book typically has a soft cover: the signatures sew directly through a folded wrapper of heavier paper (140 to 180 gsm cartridge or cover stock), or through a thin board. Paper grain must run parallel to the spine in the wrapper. For a wrapper, cut the cover long-grain with the grain running head to tail.
Preparing the stations
A herringbone pattern needs an even number of
sewing stations
per signature, arranged in angled pairs. Six stations per signature is the standard layout for an A5 book.

Mark the spine of the first signature with a pencil. Place two stations 10 mm from the head, 8 mm apart and angled so the left hole is 2 mm closer to the head than the right. The pair leans slightly head-left.
Place the next pair in the centre of the spine, angled the opposite way: left hole 2 mm closer to the tail. The pair leans tail-left.
Place the third pair 10 mm from the tail, matching the angle of the first pair. Head-left lean.

Use this signature as a template to mark and pierce all remaining signatures and the cover. Stack everything together with a piece of waste board underneath, and pierce through the whole set in one pass with the bookbinding awl. The holes line up automatically.
Sewing
Thread the needle with a length of waxed linen thread roughly four times the book's spine height. Wax the thread a second time before starting.
Start inside signature one, at the left hole of the head pair. Pass the needle out through the cover. Leave a 50 mm tail inside.
Pass back in through the right hole of the same pair. Pull the thread through so a diagonal run shows on the outside of the spine.
Move to the centre pair. Pass out through the left hole, then in through the right hole. Two diagonals showing on the spine, now leaning the opposite direction.
Repeat for the tail pair: out left, in right. Three V shapes on the spine.

Place signature two over signature one. Enter through the tail-pair left hole from outside, travelling inward through the signature. Knot around the previous thread run before moving to the centre and head pairs.

Continue across all signatures in the same order: head pair, centre pair, tail pair, working out and in through each station. The herringbone pattern builds on the outside as each signature adds its row.
After the last signature, knot the working thread around the previous run at the head and trim the tail flush with the inside of the first signature.
Finishing
The completed book has a pronounced herringbone pattern along the full spine. The
foredge

is untrimmed in most herringbone long-stitch work; the deckle edge of handmade paper or the slightly uneven foredge of folded signatures reads as part of the aesthetic. If you want a trimmed foredge, press the book in a finishing press, mark a pencil line, and cut against a steel rule with a very sharp craft knife.
Common mistakes

items.fix: Re-pierce using a stacked jig with a backing board. Even tiny misalignments accumulate across five or more signatures.
items.cause: The sewing stations were not pierced through all signatures simultaneously.
items.symptom: The herringbone pattern is uneven across signatures

items.fix: Pierce with a slightly larger awl and wax the thread twice. Linen thread without wax abrades against the paper fibres at each hole.
items.cause: The holes are too small for the thread or the thread is not waxed enough.
items.symptom: Thread breaks at the station holes

items.fix: After each pair, pull the thread firmly and hold for two seconds before moving on. Consistent tension requires deliberate slowing down.
items.cause: The thread tension is inconsistent across pairs.
items.symptom: Signatures splay open at the foredge

content.text: Where it leads

The herringbone uses the same sewing stations as a standard long-stitch. To vary the pattern, try a four-hole version. Or add a second thread colour. Pass the second thread over the spine thread between signatures to deepen the herringbone lines.
